_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Williamsburg County, SC. Records from 1804 (county formation). A relatively well-preserved courthouse with continuous records, but the county's poverty and large tenant farming population mean thinner records than the wealthier Lowcountry counties.
display_name: Williamsburg County, South Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by 1954 building. Records transferred intact.

courthouse_history.notes: Williamsburg County was formed in 1804 from parts of Georgetown District. The county has not suffered a catastrophic courthouse loss. Records are continuous from formation but relatively thin for a Lowcountry county — Williamsburg was poorer than Charleston, Georgetown, or Beaufort, and the large tenant farming population left fewer county-level records.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Williamsburg County's courthouse records have survived without catastrophic loss. The record volume is modest — the county was never as wealthy or as densely populated as the coastal Lowcountry counties, and the large Black tenant farming population generated fewer county-level records than a propertied population would.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The records survive, but their absence for a particular ancestor may reflect the ancestor's economic circumstances — tenant farmers, sharecroppers, and landless laborers left few traces in county deed and probate records.
record_losses.source: SC Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.

deeds.gaps: No major gaps. Pre-1804 land records are in Georgetown District records at SCDAH.
deeds.repository: Williamsburg County Register of Deeds; SCDAH
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com SC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Fair — fewer probate proceedings than wealthier counties because less wealth generated fewer estates.
wills_estates.gaps: Thin in the earliest decades. Loose papers fragmentary.

marriages.completeness: Statewide marriage licenses from 1911. Before that, church records — Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian — are the primary source.
marriages.gaps: Before 1911, church records and Freedmen's Bureau marriage registers required.

court_minutes.repository: Williamsburg County Clerk of Court; SCDAH

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until mid-1920s. Rural compliance was slow.

substitute_sources.description: The Williamsburg Presbyterian Church (Scots-Irish, established 1736) is one of the oldest congregations in the Pee Dee/Lowcountry transition zone. Its records — membership rolls, session minutes, baptisms — document the Scots-Irish community that settled along the Black River.
substitute_sources.repository: Williamsburg Presbyterian Church; SCDAH; Presbyterian Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for the Scots-Irish families who settled Williamsburg. Session minutes document discipline cases that reveal family and community details.
substitute_sources.source: SC Department of Archives and History.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1810–1940. Slave schedules 1850, 1860. Agricultural schedules 1850–1880.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: May be the primary systematic record for Williamsburg County's large tenant farming population, who left few county-level records.

narrative_hooks: Williamsburg County's courthouse has no major losses — {ancestor_name}'s records are likely there if {pronoun_subject} owned property or appeared in court after 1804.

If {ancestor_name} was a tenant farmer in Williamsburg County, the courthouse may hold nothing — the landless left few county records. The federal census, once a decade, may be the entire paper trail.

The Williamsburg Presbyterian Church was established in 1736 — if {ancestor_name} was Scots-Irish, the church's session minutes may record {pronoun_possessive} baptism, marriage, or discipline case decades before any county record exists.
Before 1911, marriage in Williamsburg County was a church matter, not a government one — {ancestor_name}'s wedding was recorded by the preacher, not the county clerk.
source_references: SC Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records in the South Carolina State Archives.
Edgar, Walter. South Carolina: A History. University of South Carolina Press, 1998.
Bass, Robert D. Swamp Fox. Sandlapper Publishing, 1959.
